references.title: Dropout and transfer paths: What are the risky profiles when analyzing university persistence with machine learning techniques?

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Dropping out of a program at the end of the second year
extracted.Courses: 54 programmes at the University of Oviedo
extracted.Data sources:
Socio-Demographic Variables,
Prior Academic History, 
Questionnaire for Academic Performance, Motivation abd Satisfaction with University
extracted.Features:
[Number_FamilyMembers, StudyHours_PerWeek, WorkHours_PerWeek, HouseWorkHours_PerWeek, Age, BACH_FP, GEN, Grade, Area_Code, Enrollment_Credits2010, Attended_cred_2010, Passed_Credits_2010, Enrollment_Credtis_2011, Attended_credits_T_2011, 
Passed_Credits_2011, Place_of_Residence, Studies_Choice_Reason, Prior_Guidance, Inyrgration_Activities_Utility, Participation_Integration_Activities, Class_Attendance, Approppriate_Methodology, RatioGradesEffort, Difficulty, Teacher_Relation, JoinNonAcademic__activities, Would_recommend_uniovi, Gender, Nationality_Code, Disability_Code, Fam_Code, Father_Stud_Code, Mother_Study_Code, Father_job, Mother_job, Acces_Way, Center_Code, Priority, First_Year_Grant_Code, Grant_2011]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors perform outlier detection and ultimately decide that they are legitimate values and, for that reason reason, should be kept.
extracted.Models:
CART, 
C4.5, 
SVM, 
Bayes Net,
Random Forest
extracted.Comments:
Authors do an ex-post analysis of dropout at their university- Not ondly do they create a set of predictive models but also look to analyze factors that help explain Dropout.

Class change is much more difficult to prediuct correctly than the otyher 2 classes. 

Authors also look to optimize their C4.5 Decision Tree (which in this case, since its WeKA should be J48).
extracted.Limitations:
Bejhhavioral data is extracted only from Self-Reports.

Small Sample size (1055 students).

Machine Learning Models used are relatively simple, but 3 of them are Tree-Based.

annotations.text: Analyzing dropout in large datasets is difficult. Authors propose a non-parametric ML approach that handles large datasets well and is interpretable. Results of this approach show that performance in first year, personal and contextual variables play a key role in predicting dropout.
